rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute diverticulitis with localized peritonism (guarding, positive rebound in the LIF), high fever with rigors, resting tachycardia and inability to tolerate oral intake — systemic sepsis features on a background of known diverticulosis. This needs urgent Emergency Department assessment for abdominal examination, inflammatory bloods, IV antibiotics, IV hydration and contrast CT to exclude/grade complications (abscess, perforation). The patient is not yet shocked and has no generalized (board-like) rigidity, so 000 ambulance is reserved for deterioration; the source arranged direct ED transfer with 000 held for perforation/septic decline. Baseline therefore T4 (ED, hours) with T5 escalation edges. Under-triage caution applies: anything below urgent same-day in-person assessment misses a possible surgical abdomen.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain suddenly spreads across the entire abdomen, or a sudden tearing/pop sensation followed by worsening generalized pain (free perforation / generalized peritonitis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Sudden generalization of pain heralds free perforation and faeculent peritonitis — a surgical emergency requiring ambulance transport and emergency theatre.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain suddenly spreads across your whole belly or you feel a tearing or popping sensation, call 000 for an ambulance straight away — do not wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Confusion, drowsiness, feeling faint, clammy or that you might collapse (septic shock)
escalation_edges.rationale: New confusion or pre-syncope on a febrile intra-abdominal infection signals septic shock and needs emergency ambulance care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become confused, drowsy, faint or clammy, call 000 immediately and tell them you have a severe abdominal infection.

escalation_edges.rationale: Feculent vomiting suggests bowel obstruction/perforation and is a surgical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you vomit dark, foul-smelling material, call 000 for an ambulance right away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Ongoing severe pain and fever with inability to keep any fluids down while awaiting or arranging assessment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same presentation — proceed to the Emergency Department now
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic upset with failed oral intake cannot be managed at home; hospital IV antibiotics, fluids and CT are required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because you have a high fever, severe pain and can't keep fluids down, you need to go to the Emergency Department now for a drip, antibiotics and a scan.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Bedbound, guarding, unable to stand upright and needing help to mobilise
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Arrange assisted/urgent transport to the Emergency Department; if pain generalizes, confusion develops or he collapses, switch to a 000 ambulance rather than private transport.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Anxious spouse present and involved in care
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the spouse the explicit 000 escalation triggers and NBM instruction, since the patient is bedbound and relying on them to act on deterioration.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person urgently — go to the Emergency Department now for an abdominal examination, blood tests, antibiotics through a drip and a CT scan. Do not eat any solid food; small sips of clear fluid only if extremely thirsty. Your spouse should call 000 for an ambulance immediately if the pain suddenly spreads across your whole belly, if you become confused or faint, or if you vomit dark foul-smelling material.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend the Emergency Department now (arranged/assisted transport); call 000 if any emergency trigger occurs
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This cannot wait for clinic hours — the Emergency Department is open 24 hours; if deterioration occurs, 000 is available at any time.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Suspected acute diverticulitis with systemic features and localized peritonism, referred to ED for CT and IV antibiotics. GP to arrange outpatient colonoscopy after recovery to exclude colorectal malignancy and review dietary/fibre measures.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a febrile localized peritonitic acute abdomen as a routine/prompt-GP problem or self-care (e.g. 'flare of known diverticulosis' or constipation).
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated pericolic infection progresses overnight to abscess, free perforation, faeculent peritonitis and septic shock.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Urgent same-day in-person assessment without ensuring ED-level IV-antibiotic care and CT, so the systemic sepsis features and surgical mimic are under-served.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Defaulting to a 000 ambulance for a haemodynamically stable, localized presentation without a generalized-peritonitis or shock trigger — appropriate on deterioration but over-triage as the baseline.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether this is uncomplicated diverticulitis or complicated disease (abscess/perforation) cannot be determined remotely without examination, bloods and CT.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as complicated-until-proven-otherwise: escalate for urgent in-person/ED assessment, imaging and IV antibiotics, and give clear 000 triggers for deterioration.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assuming uncomplicated disease and managing at home with oral antibiotics/analgesia, or reassuring it as a benign flare.
